access.accessMethod: Read the exact pinned source as raw XML, in the GitHub blob view, or in the checked-out CME submodule
access.entityLabel: General Corpus approved source XML for Paston
access.format: application/xml

entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow, supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule and selected by the publication source override.
entities.label: General Corpus approved source XML for Paston
notes: The file is 1,389,245 bytes at the recorded CME submodule commit.
CME/source/CME_from_OTA/Paston.xml is byte-identical at that commit, but the approved PDF colophon names the recent_changes/repairs_2023 path; exact-path provenance is therefore preserved.
It contains 15 DIV1, 423 DIV2, 9 DIV3, 670 PB, 200 FOREIGN, 715 DEL, and 589 UNCLEAR elements.

rights.basis: The file itself contains legacy use language but no clear file-specific license grant; the CME repository's Unlicense text is framed for software and was not assumed to license this modern transcription or edition-derived markup.
rights.component: The repository XML artifact as a distinct digital file
notes: Do not infer rights in the 1971 editorial transcription from the public-domain status of the medieval documents.

access.accessMethod: U-M item page, table of contents, and full-text section browser
access.entityLabel: University of Michigan CME digital text Paston
access.format: HTML full text

rights.basis: The current U-M collection page states that collection materials are public domain in the United States and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
rights.component: The Paston digital text and materials presented in the current U-M CME collection
notes: The individual Paston text embeds older personal-scholarly-use language. That conflict should be resolved with U-M before redistribution of the complete encoding.
No Copyright–United States is not an affirmative worldwide license.

entities.description: The public U-M structured text of Davis's Part I, with author-group and document navigation and page milestones through printed p. 670.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text Paston
notes: The XML editorial statement says printed front and back matter and printed editorial notes were omitted.
The representation retains the edition title page, document text, headings, deletions, unclear readings, foreign-language spans, and page milestones for pp. 1–670.

U-M's collection history says its initial mid-1990s texts came from OTA, U-M transcription, or faculty contributions; this item's embedded U-1685-C identifier supports an OTA connection but does not by itself document each transformation step.

rights.basis: The current U-M collection page states that collection materials are public domain in the United States and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
rights.component: The Paston digital text and materials presented in the current U-M CME collection
notes: The individual Paston text embeds older personal-scholarly-use language. That conflict should be resolved with U-M before redistribution of the complete encoding.
No Copyright–United States is not an affirmative worldwide license.

access.accessMethod: OTA item page and direct SGML/header downloads
access.entityLabel: Oxford Text Archive ota:1685 SGML text
access.format: SGML text and XML metadata header

workPortion.label: The entire Part I SGML collection

rights.basis: The current OTA rights metadata says use is restricted, usually to non-commercial use with prior depositor permission and inclusion of the complete header with distributed copies.
rights.component: OTA 1685 SGML text and header
notes: Public download availability does not itself grant unrestricted redistribution or commercial reuse.

entities.description: The current OTA Core Collection object exposing a complete SGML text and separate metadata header for the Part I collection.
entities.label: Oxford Text Archive ota:1685 SGML text
notes: The SGML is 1,381,115 bytes and is not byte-identical to the U-M/CME XML; it uses a different structure and begins its author-group sequence differently.
The current record dates the collection 1425–1503 and identifies Middle English and the Paston Family.
Its header calls 1685 a revised version of OTA 0395 and records a 1992-04-03 deposit by Norman Davis.

The header sourceDesc instead names an E. V. Gordon 1953 two-volume edition. That conflicts with the SGML's opening edition statement naming Norman Davis, Oxford, Clarendon Press, 1971, and is preserved as unresolved inherited metadata rather than accepted as the source.

rights.basis: The current OTA rights metadata says use is restricted, usually to non-commercial use with prior depositor permission and inclusion of the complete header with distributed copies.
rights.component: OTA 1685 SGML text and header
notes: Public download availability does not itself grant unrestricted redistribution or commercial reuse.

access.accessMethod: OTA item page and direct downloads of the complete three-part legacy text plus metadata header
access.entityLabel: Oxford Text Archive ota:0395 three-part legacy text
access.format: Three text/plain files and one XML metadata header

workPortion.label: Part 1 of the complete three-file encoding
locators: Agnes Paston through the first portion of John Paston III

workPortion.label: Part 2 of the complete three-file encoding
Part 3 of the complete three-file encoding
locators: Margery Paston and William Paston IV

notes: The three text files together are the complete published OTA 0395 text object; no one file is a complete standalone representation of the collection.

rights.basis: The exact OTA 0395 header states distribution by the University of Oxford under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported.
rights.component: OTA 0395 metadata header and three-part legacy text as distributed by Oxford
notes: The NonCommercial condition matters for profit-oriented print-on-demand use.

This provider assertion does not resolve rights in Davis's 1971 editorial contributions, and it conflicts operationally with OTA 1685's later prior-permission wording; do not merge the two access layers.

entities.description: The public older OTA representation of the Part I collection in three plain-text files with COCOA references and undocumented legacy conventions; OTA 1685 identifies itself as this object's revised SGML version.
entities.label: Oxford Text Archive ota:0395 three-part legacy text
notes: The header records Norman Davis as creator of the machine-readable version, a 1984-10-30 deposit, and three files totaling 1,307,131 bytes.
The header calls the representation legacy, duplicate, and superseded by OTA 1685; its three files begin with Agnes Paston and retain COCOA-style conventions.

Like the 1685 header, its sourceDesc names E. V. Gordon and 1953. The actual text, creator/depositor metadata, U-M/CME artifact, and later reissue evidence instead identify Davis's 1971 Part I, so the conflicting sourceDesc is not promoted to a source-edition entity.

rights.basis: The exact OTA 0395 header states distribution by the University of Oxford under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported.
rights.component: OTA 0395 metadata header and three-part legacy text as distributed by Oxford
notes: The NonCommercial condition matters for profit-oriented print-on-demand use.

This provider assertion does not resolve rights in Davis's 1971 editorial contributions, and it conflicts operationally with OTA 1685's later prior-permission wording; do not merge the two access layers.

bibliographic.citation: Norman Davis, ed., Paston Letters and Papers of the Fifteenth Century, Part I (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1971).
bibliographic.title: Paston Letters and Papers of the Fifteenth Century, Part I

entities.description: Davis's edited Part I collection of documents produced by the Pastons, arranged in fifteen family-member groups and numbered 1–421 with additional items 122A and 354A.
entities.label: Norman Davis, Paston Letters and Papers, Part I (1971)
notes: The U-M/CME text tracks printed pp. 1–670 but omits the print front and back matter and editorial notes.
Davis differentiates autographs, drafts, dictated texts, and scribal copies; authorship, scribal hand, and physical object are not interchangeable.

rights.basis: The historical documents were created in the fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries; U-M also applies No Copyright–United States to its collection presentation.
rights.component: Underlying fifteenth-century documentary wording, excluding Davis's modern selection, transcription choices, notes, and typography
notes: This finding does not extend to modern editorial reconstruction, markup, or manuscript photography.

rights.basis: A work first published in 1971 can have a maximum U.S. term of 95 years from publication if copyright was secured and maintained; no item-specific clearance or renewal analysis was completed.
rights.component: Davis's 1971 selection, arrangement, modern editorial transcription, notes, and other protectable editorial matter
notes: 2066 is the conservative maximum-term endpoint, not a finding that every feature is protected until then.

next_steps: Consult Beadle and Richmond's Part III complete concordance and Davis's Part I manuscript apparatus.
Verify every concordance entry against current British Library and other repository catalogs before adding item-level relations.
Represent many-to-many mappings rather than assuming one Davis number equals one physical document.
openQuestions.question: What is the exhaustive document-to-current-shelfmark and folio concordance for every Part I unit, including dispersed, missing, copied, and multi-state documents?

next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services to confirm which complete volumes were digitized and the restoration timetable.

Submit the exact order form and request condition and fee quotations for each complete physical volume, including bindings, flyleaves, blanks, every surface, endorsements, seals, labels, and adjacent non-Part-I content.
Seek separate written terms from the published service and permissions contacts for private research, repository linking, publication, and redistribution before sharing any supplied images.
notes: No enquiry or order response is recorded in this manifest, so this question is unresolved rather than pending an evidenced external response.
openQuestions.question: When will complete page-image access to the digitized Paston manuscript volumes be restored, or can the British Library supply complete lawful research reproductions now?

next_steps: Request production records or archived headers from U-M and OTA.
Perform a document-level structural and textual diff among all three OTA 0395 parts, current OTA 1685 SGML, U-M output, and both CME paths.
Treat OTA's confirmed 0395-to-1685 revision separately from the still-undocumented 1685-to-U-M transformation.
Do not upgrade version_of to copied_from or encoded_from between U-M and OTA until direction and intermediates are documented.
openQuestions.question: What exact file, date, and transformation process connect OTA 0395, OTA U-1685-C/current 1685, the U-M CME item, and the byte-identical CME repository siblings?

next_steps: Ask OTA whether the Gordon sourceDesc was inherited from an unrelated or superseded catalog record and request corrected item metadata.
Compare the cached 0395 and 1685 files against a lawful Davis 1971 copy before asserting exact textual coverage at line level.
Do not add an E. V. Gordon source-edition node unless independent bibliographic and textual evidence establishes one.
openQuestions.question: Why do the OTA 0395 and 1685 headers name an E. V. Gordon 1953 two-volume source when their creator/depositor metadata, digital text, and U-M/CME representation identify Norman Davis's 1971 Part I edition?

next_steps: Ask U-M and OTA for item-specific written clarification covering source code repositories, non-commercial research, print-on-demand, and commercial reuse.
Ask OTA which terms govern transformed or combined use across 0395 and 1685 rather than assuming the older license overrides the later record.
Separate underlying medieval wording, Davis's editorial contributions, U-M/OTA markup, and provider-supplied scans in every permission request.
Do not publish cached OTA or controlled edition files merely because they are technically downloadable.
openQuestions.question: Which current terms govern redistribution and commercial reuse of the U-M and OTA Paston encodings given U-M's No Copyright–United States collection statement, embedded legacy restrictions, OTA 0395's CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 assertion, OTA 1685's prior-permission language, and the potentially protected 1971 edition?

next_steps: Use Part III's concordance to enumerate every non-major-volume holding and current shelfmark.
Locate an exact current Norfolk Record Office catalog record before modeling Walter Rye MS 38.
Add lost or unlocated witnesses explicitly rather than forcing them into the major-volume entities.
openQuestions.question: Which Part I originals or supporting documents are held outside the eleven major British Library volumes, including Norfolk Record Office Walter Rye MS 38 and scattered British Library charters and manuscripts?

reviewNotes: The XML's 423 top-level editorial containers, the scholarly count of 422 authored documents, and the number of physical medieval documents are separate metrics.
The eleven British Library witness entities are major bound volumes with catalog-confirmed Davis-number content, not an exhaustive inventory of every Part I source.

The exact U-M-to-OTA conversion history is not documented; current files demonstrate related textual content but materially different SGML/XML structures and orderings. OTA itself confirms only that 1685 revises 0395.

The OTA 0395 and 1685 headers inherit an E. V. Gordon 1953 source description that conflicts with their deposited Paston text files, depositor/creator metadata, and the U-M/CME source statement naming Norman Davis's 1971 edition.

Rights claims conflict across layers: U-M presents the collection as No Copyright–United States, the Paston item embeds older personal-scholarly-use language, OTA 0395 asserts CC BY-NC-SA 3.0, and OTA 1685 states non-commercial/prior-permission restrictions.

lineage.summary: The approved General Corpus artifact is the exact Paston.xml file recorded in the generated PDF colophon. It is a repository copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse text, whose header identifies Oxford Text Archive U-1685-C and Norman Davis's 1971 Part I edition. Current OTA 1685 exposes a complete SGML text and identifies itself as a revised version of the still-public three-file OTA 0395 encoding; neither OTA representation is byte-equivalent to the U-M/CME XML. Both OTA headers contain a conflicting E. V. Gordon 1953 source description even though the deposited texts and U-M/CME artifact identify Davis 1971, so that inherited header claim is recorded but not used as the textual source. Davis's edition transcribes a dispersed archive, not one manuscript: eleven major British Library bound volumes are recorded here, while an exhaustive document-to-witness concordance remains future work. Complete legacy digital coverage is reported for several Paston volumes, but current British Library catalog records say those images are unavailable. The complete OTA 0395 and 1685 source objects, a complete scholarly article, a partial 2004-edition preview, and supporting metadata are cached; no complete manuscript-volume facsimile was obtainable on 2026-07-14.

catalogSummary.title: Paston Letters and Papers of the Fifteenth Century, Part I

catalogingSubject.label: Paston Letters and Papers of the Fifteenth Century, Part I
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Norman Davis's Part I editorial collection of letters and papers produced by members of the Paston family and their associated clerks and documentary creators, represented by 423 top-level document containers in the repository XML. The cataloging subject is not one letter, one authorial work, one physical manuscript, the whole surviving Paston archive, Part II (documents addressed to the Pastons), or Part III. The medieval documents, Davis's edition, the Oxford Text Archive and University of Michigan encodings, the repository XML, and the many surviving manuscript volumes remain distinct layers.

notes: The U-M header's 1425–1496 note is too narrow: Davis no. 391 is headed 1500? and no. 392 probably after April 1503. The final numbered item is earlier because the edition is grouped by family member rather than strict chronology.

scope.description: Dates of the historical documents represented in Part I
Davis's editorial compilation and arrangement of Part I
First publication of Davis's Part I edition

extent.description: The repository XML has 15 DIV1 author groups, 423 DIV2 document containers, 9 nested DIV3 subdivisions, and page milestones for printed pp. 1–670. Its labels comprise integer Davis nos. 1–421 plus 122A and 354A, with no missing integers. This is an editorial/encoding extent, not a physical-document count or a clean authored-letter count; a scholarly linguistic study independently reports 422 authored documents. No LG or L elements occur, although one document contains a short verse draft embedded in prose-oriented documentary markup.

formStatements.description: A predominantly prose documentary collection. A short verse draft occurs within a document, but the XML contains no structured verse groups or lines and the incidental passage does not make the whole collection mixed-form.

scope.description: Legal and administrative records are recurrent components of the collection, not a claim that every item has this genre
Household and estate papers are recurrent components of the collection, not a claim that every item has this genre
Principal language of the correspondence and documentary texts

languageStatements.label: Anglo-Norman French
notes: The XML has 33 FOREIGN elements explicitly labeled FR; individual passages require item-level review before finer linguistic classification.

scope.description: French occurs as embedded words, phrases, and documentary passages across the collection rather than as the language of every item

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The XML has 19 FOREIGN elements labeled LAT and many additional unlabelled foreign-language spans; the unlabelled spans are not automatically classified here.

scope.description: Latin occurs as embedded legal formulae, documentary passages, words, and phrases across the collection
Modern editorial headings, notes, expansions, and descriptive apparatus

notes: The exact approved publication source is CME/source/recent_changes/repairs_2023/Paston.xml. A byte-identical CME_from_OTA/Paston.xml sibling exists at the pinned submodule commit, but it is not substituted for the colophon-recorded source path.

The full public OTA 1685 SGML/header and predecessor OTA 0395 header/three-part text, a complete public research article, a partial 2004 edition preview, Internet Archive metadata, the British Library price list, and supporting rights material are cached under source-cache/Paston/. No complete manuscript-volume facsimile could be downloaded because the British Library's legacy full digital coverage is currently unavailable.

The OTA headers' E. V. Gordon 1953 source description conflicts with their own Davis creator/depositor data, the OTA 1685 SGML edition statement, and the U-M/CME source statement. It is retained only as unresolved digital-catalog history and does not alter the cataloged Davis 1971 source edition.
Part I is treated as a collection and its fifteen author-group sections as a summary structure; this manifest is not an item-level catalog of all 423 editorial containers.

next_steps: Use the complete concordance in Part III and Davis's Part I introductions and notes to assign stable item records.
Record authorial, scribal, editorial, and physical-object counts separately, especially for 122A, 354A, copies, drafts, and multi-part documents.
openQuestions.question: How should the XML's 423 editorial containers, the scholarly count of 422 authored documents, and the count of surviving or lost physical documents be reconciled item by item?

next_steps: Consult Part III's complete concordance to the original manuscripts and reconcile every entry against current repository catalogs.
Add item-level many-to-many mappings without assuming that one Davis number equals one physical object.
openQuestions.question: What is the exhaustive current shelfmark and folio crosswalk for every Part I editorial unit, including surviving, missing, and dispersed originals?

next_steps: Review Davis's item notes and complete manuscript images before propagating collective authorship or language labels.
Distinguish personal composition, dictation, clerkly drafting, copying, issuance, and later endorsement.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact item-level languages, creators, scribes, documentary states, and composition dates?

scope.description: Principal family, estate, household, and political setting
Regional context of the Paston family archive and its language
London is a recurring associated place across the collection, although not every document concerns or was produced there

notes: The schema role author is broad documentary responsibility. Davis distinguishes autographs, drafts, dictated letters, and scribal copies; the collection also includes non-family documentary matter.

scope.description: Documents produced by, for, or in the names of the family members under whom Davis groups Part I

responsibilities.agentName: Associated clerks, copyists, legal writers, and documentary creators

scope.description: Copied, drafted, issued, or mediated documentary texts not safely reducible to personal Paston authorship
Selection, arrangement, transcription, annotation, and publication of Part I
Part I boundary, author-group arrangement, document numbering, and dating
The historical-document text of Davis nos. 1–421 plus 122A and 354A, as distinct from editorial apparatus

subjects.label: Late Middle English language and scribal variation

summaries.value: Davis's Part I gathers letters and papers produced by the Pastons, a Norfolk gentry family, and by associated clerks and documentary creators. Its correspondence, memoranda, wills, inventories, petitions, legal instruments, estate papers, and household records illuminate family relationships, property management, litigation, local and royal politics, war, travel, material life, and changes in fifteenth-century written English. The documents are arranged in fifteen family-member groups rather than a single chronology and run chiefly from 1425 to 1503. The collection is a modern scholarly construction from a dispersed archive: its 423 XML containers and Davis numbers should not be mistaken for 423 uniform authored letters or physical manuscripts.

The repository XML contains fifteen top-level author-group sections and 423 document containers: Davis nos. 1–421 plus 122A and 354A. The groups are William Paston I (12), Agnes Paston (22), John Paston I (44), Edmond Paston (2), William Paston II (33), Clement Paston (7), Elizabeth Poynings or Browne, née Paston (4), Margaret Paston (107), John Paston II (86), John Paston III (78), Edmond Paston II (8), Walter Paston (4), William Paston III (9), Margery Paston (6), and William Paston IV (1).

The 423 top-level XML containers are editorial and encoding units. A linguistic study describes 422 authored documents, and the surviving archive is distributed among many individual documents and bound volumes. The discrepancy is preserved because 122A is a copied royal writ and 354A is a mediated draft, while other editorial units may also differ from physical-object or authorship counts. Item-level manuscript shelfmarks and creator roles therefore require a concordance rather than arithmetic inference.

Its correspondence, memoranda, wills, inventories, petitions, legal instruments, estate papers, and household records illuminate family relationships, property management, litigation, local and royal politics, war, travel, material life, and changes in fifteenth-century written English. The documents are arranged in fifteen family-member groups rather than a single chronology and run chiefly from 1425 to 1503. The collection is a modern scholarly construction from a dispersed archive: its 423 XML containers and Davis numbers should not be mistaken for 423 uniform authored letters or physical manuscripts.
